connections.name: HDMI 1.4 + USB 3.0
requirements: HDMI 1.4 output (or DisplayPort with HDMI adapter)
Additional USB 3.0 ports for each Constellation sensor

knownIssues.title: Legacy Oculus runtime — no longer updated
knownIssues.description: The Rift CV1 requires the legacy Oculus PC app runtime. Meta ended official support in 2021; new software features and bug fixes no longer target this device.
knownIssues.workaround: Use SteamVR via the compatibility layer. Check that OVRServer_x64 still launches successfully — if not, reinstall the Oculus Rift legacy driver from archived installers.

knownIssues.title: HDMI output required — modern GPUs may need an adapter
knownIssues.description: Many current GPUs (RTX 40/50 series) have only one HDMI port. The CV1 needs HDMI 1.4, and DisplayPort→HDMI active adapters can introduce black-screen or audio sync issues.
knownIssues.workaround: Use a validated active DP→HDMI adapter (Club3D / StarTech). Avoid passive adapters.

knownIssues.title: Constellation sensors saturate USB bandwidth
knownIssues.description: Each external sensor consumes a USB 3.0 port and significant bandwidth. On single-controller motherboards, plugging headset + 2 sensors + Touch USB receiver into the same controller causes tracking dropouts.
knownIssues.workaround: Distribute sensors across different USB host controllers. Use a PCIe USB expansion card (Inateck / StarTech) if the motherboard only has one controller.

optimizationTips.tip: Keep supersampling at 1.0× — CV1 is pixel-limited, not GPU-limited
optimizationTips.simpleExplanation: The Rift CV1 has a modest 1080×1200 per eye display. Pushing supersampling above 1.0× offers diminishing returns and stresses your GPU unnecessarily.
optimizationTips.advancedExplanation: At SteamVR SS 1.0×, native render is 1232×1344 per eye. Anything above 1.2× wastes GPU cycles on detail the OLED can't resolve. Instead, max out in-app rendering quality (shadows, textures, anti-aliasing).
